quiz_questions.question_text: A scatterplot displays the relationship between:

options.text: One variable and time
Frequency and value
Two quantitative variables
Two categorical variables

quiz_questions.explanation: Each point represents paired measurements of two quantitative variables.

quiz_questions.question_text: r = 0.95 indicates:

options.text: No correlation
Strong positive linear correlation
Strong negative correlation
Weak correlation

quiz_questions.explanation: r close to 1 means a strong positive linear relationship.

quiz_questions.question_text: The correlation coefficient r ranges from:

options.text: −∞ to ∞
0 to 1
−1 to 1

quiz_questions.explanation: The correlation coefficient is bounded between −1 and 1.

quiz_questions.question_text: r = −0.85 indicates:

options.text: Weak positive correlation
Perfect positive correlation
No correlation
Strong negative linear correlation

quiz_questions.explanation: Negative r near −1 means a strong negative linear relationship.

quiz_questions.question_text: r = 1 means:

options.text: The variables are independent
Perfect positive linear relationship
No correlation
Strong negative correlation

quiz_questions.explanation: Every point falls exactly on a line with positive slope.

quiz_questions.question_text: r only measures __ relationships:

options.text: Exponential
Curved
Linear
All types of

quiz_questions.explanation: r captures only the linear component; nonlinear patterns require other measures.

quiz_questions.question_text: An outlier can:

options.text: Strongly influence r
Only increase r
Never affect r
Only decrease r

quiz_questions.explanation: Outliers can pull r toward 0 or inflate it, depending on their position.

quiz_questions.question_text: If r² = 0.81, then r could be:

options.text: 0.656 only
0.81 only
0.405 only

quiz_questions.explanation: r = ±√0.81 = ±0.9; the sign depends on the direction of the relationship.

quiz_questions.question_text: Changing units (e.g., cm to meters) affects r:

options.text: No, r is unitless and unaffected
Yes, it changes r
Only for negative correlations
Only if both variables change units

quiz_questions.explanation: r is standardized and unaffected by linear changes of units.

quiz_questions.question_text: If every data point falls exactly on a line with negative slope, r = ?
quiz_questions.explanation: Perfect negative linear relationship means r = −1.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the correlation coefficient?

options.text: A measure of the center of a dataset
A numerical measure of the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two quantitative variables
The difference between the maximum and minimum values
The slope of the regression line

quiz_questions.explanation: The correlation coefficient r quantifies how tightly data cluster around a straight line and in which direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does r² (the coefficient of determination) represent?

options.text: The probability that the relationship is real
The slope of the regression line squared
The proportion of the variation in y that is explained by the linear relationship with x
The number of data points squared

quiz_questions.explanation: r² tells us what fraction of the total variability in the response variable is accounted for by the linear model.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a scatterplot?

options.text: A line graph connecting consecutive values over time
A graph in which each data point represents a pair of values for two quantitative variables, plotted on x- and y-axes
A bar chart showing frequencies
A circle divided into sectors

quiz_questions.explanation: A scatterplot uses the coordinate plane to display paired quantitative observations.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean for a correlation to be 'positive'?

options.text: The data has no outliers
As one variable increases, the other tends to increase as well
The correlation is strong
Both variables are always positive numbers

quiz_questions.explanation: A positive correlation means the two variables move in the same direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'correlation does not imply causation' mean?

options.text: Just because two variables are correlated does not mean that one causes the other to change
Correlation is always zero
Causation always comes before correlation
Only experiments can show correlation

quiz_questions.explanation: A lurking variable or coincidence may explain the association; correlation alone cannot establish a cause-and-effect link.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by the 'direction' of a scatterplot?

options.text: Whether the overall pattern rises (positive) or falls (negative) from left to right
Whether the points are close together or far apart
The number of clusters in the data
The steepness of the regression line

quiz_questions.explanation: Direction describes whether the association between the two variables is positive (upward trend) or negative (downward trend).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the 'form' of a scatterplot describe?

options.text: Whether the pattern is linear, curved, or has no clear shape
The total number of data points
The units of the axes
The range of the x-values

quiz_questions.explanation: Form refers to the overall shape of the point cloud, such as linear, curved, or scattered with no pattern.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by the 'strength' of a correlation?

options.text: How closely the data points cluster around a straight line, measured by |r|
How many data points exist in the dataset
Whether r is positive or negative
The steepness of the best-fit line

quiz_questions.explanation: Strength is determined by the absolute value of r; values closer to 1 indicate a tighter linear pattern.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the explanatory variable in a scatterplot?

options.text: The variable plotted on the y-axis that we want to predict
The variable with the largest standard deviation
The variable plotted on the x-axis that may help explain or predict the response
Any variable that is categorical

quiz_questions.explanation: By convention the explanatory (independent) variable is placed on the x-axis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean when r = 0?

options.text: The variables have the same mean
The data was collected incorrectly
The two variables are completely unrelated in every way
There is no linear relationship, though a nonlinear relationship may still exist

quiz_questions.explanation: r = 0 indicates no linear trend; the variables could still have a strong curved relationship.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher records daily ice cream sales and daily drowning incidents over a summer. She finds r = 0.87. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: Drownings cause people to buy ice cream
A lurking variable such as hot weather likely drives both variables up, producing a strong positive correlation that does not imply causation
The correlation must be incorrect because these variables are unrelated
Eating ice cream causes drownings

quiz_questions.explanation: Temperature is a confounding variable that increases both ice cream sales and swimming activity, illustrating that correlation does not imply causation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A biology class measures the heights and shoe sizes of 50 students and calculates r = 0.72 with r² = 0.52. Which statement is correct?

options.text: 52% of students have large shoe sizes
Height causes shoe size to increase
52% of the variation in shoe size is explained by the linear relationship with height
72% of the variation in shoe size is explained by height

quiz_questions.explanation: r² = 0.52 means 52% of the variability in shoe size is accounted for by the linear association with height.

quiz_questions.question_text: An economist plots unemployment rate versus consumer confidence index for 30 countries. The scatterplot slopes downward with r = −0.78. What does this suggest?

options.text: There is no meaningful relationship
Lower consumer confidence causes higher unemployment
78% of countries have low unemployment
Countries with lower unemployment tend to have higher consumer confidence

quiz_questions.explanation: r = −0.78 indicates a fairly strong negative linear association: as unemployment rises, consumer confidence tends to fall.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher records hours of study and test scores for her class and finds r = 0.65. She then converts study hours from hours to minutes. What happens to r?

options.text: r increases because the numbers are larger
r stays at 0.65 because r is unitless and unaffected by linear unit changes
r decreases because the scale changed
r becomes 0.65 × 60

quiz_questions.explanation: Because r is calculated from standardized values, changing units through a linear transformation does not alter it.

quiz_questions.question_text: A data analyst notices that a dataset of car age (years) versus resale value ($) has r = −0.91. One car is a rare vintage model worth far more than predicted. If that car is removed, what is most likely to happen to |r|?

options.text: |r| will decrease because removing data always weakens correlation
|r| will remain exactly the same
r will become positive
|r| will likely increase because the outlier was weakening the linear pattern

quiz_questions.explanation: An outlier that deviates from the overall linear trend pulls r toward 0; removing it typically strengthens the measured linear relationship.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student plots running speed (mph) versus heart rate (bpm) for 20 athletes and sees a clear curved (U-shaped) pattern. She calculates r = 0.12. Why is r so low despite the obvious pattern?

options.text: r = 0.12 is actually a strong correlation
Heart rate is not a quantitative variable
r only measures linear associations; the U-shaped curve is not captured by r
The data must have been entered incorrectly

quiz_questions.explanation: r measures only the linear component of an association; a symmetric curve can produce r near 0 despite a clear nonlinear pattern.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical company tests a new drug and reports r = 0.45 between dosage and symptom improvement. A newspaper headline claims 'Drug causes improvement.' What is wrong with this claim?

options.text: Correlation alone cannot establish causation; without a controlled experiment, confounding factors could explain the association
Dosage is a categorical variable, so r is invalid
r = 0.45 is too small to matter
The correlation should be negative for the drug to be effective

quiz_questions.explanation: Without a properly controlled experiment, confounders such as patient health or placebo effects could produce the observed correlation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A fitness trainer collects data on weekly exercise hours (x) and resting heart rate (y) for 40 clients and finds r = −0.68 and r² = 0.46. Which real-world statement is most accurate?

options.text: Clients who exercise more always have the lowest resting heart rate
Exercise explains 68% of the variation in resting heart rate
About 46% of the variation in resting heart rate is explained by the linear relationship with exercise hours
46% of clients exercise regularly

quiz_questions.explanation: r² = 0.46 means 46% of the variability in resting heart rate among these clients is accounted for by the linear association with exercise hours.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two students calculate r for the same dataset: Student A uses (height in inches, weight in pounds) and Student B uses (height in centimeters, weight in kilograms). Their r values will be:

options.text: Different because the units are different
Cannot be determined without knowing the data
Exactly the same because r is not affected by linear changes in units
Student B's r will be larger because the metric system uses larger numbers for height

quiz_questions.explanation: r is computed from standardized (z-score) values, making it invariant to linear changes in scale.

quiz_questions.question_text: A city planner plots average commute time (x) versus residents' self-reported stress level (y) for 25 neighborhoods. The scatterplot shows a moderately strong upward trend with r = 0.61. One neighborhood near a park has very low stress despite a long commute. This point is best described as:

options.text: A point that will have no effect on r
A data entry error that should always be deleted
An outlier that does not follow the overall trend and may have a special explanation such as proximity to a park
Evidence that commute time and stress are unrelated

quiz_questions.explanation: An individual observation that departs from the overall pattern is an outlier; investigating its context (e.g., park proximity) may explain the deviation.
